findings_details.title: resolve_quality_dir doc claims "pure query" but sibling functions have mkdir side effects
findings_details.body: The module header and resolve_quality_dir's docstring describe the API as a "pure query" that "Does NOT create the directory" (lines 14-16). resolve_proof_dir (line 27) and resolve_plans_dir (line 35) then call mkdir -p as a side effect on every call. Mixing pure and side-effectful resolvers under the same naming convention surprises callers who read the header and expect all three to behave the same way. Either rename the side-effectful pair (e.g., ensure_proof_dir / ensure_plans_dir) or split the API into resolve_*_dir (pure) + ensure_*_dir (creates). At minimum, update the module header to document that two of the three resolvers create on call.

findings_details.title: CHECKPOINT_DIR allowed as setter but not recognized as rooted prefix
findings_details.body: SETTER_ASSIGNMENT_RE (line 42-45) accepts PROOF_DIR, QUALITY_DIR, METRICS_DIR, and CHECKPOINT_DIR as setter targets. ROOTED_PREFIX_RE (line 25-31) allows usage via $REPO_ROOT, $PROOF_DIR, $QUALITY_DIR, $METRICS_DIR — but NOT $CHECKPOINT_DIR. The gate therefore permits a line like CHECKPOINT_DIR=".quality/checkpoints" but will flag any later use such as "$CHECKPOINT_DIR/foo.json" even though that is the intended rooted idiom run-gates.sh itself uses (line 8: CHECKPOINT_DIR="${CHECKPOINT_DIR:-.quality/checkpoints}"). The two lists should be symmetric: if a variable is a legitimate setter target it must also be a legitimate rooted prefix. Add $CHECKPOINT_DIR to ROOTED_PREFIX_RE, or remove CHECKPOINT_DIR from SETTER_ASSIGNMENT_RE.

findings_details.title: ROOTED_PREFIX_RE accepts four lowercase aliases not documented as canonical
findings_details.body: Lines 29-30 permit $git_root, $repo_root, $local_root, and $worktree_root as rooted prefixes, but the gate's own header (lines 4-7) and the broader skill-authoring convention document REPO_ROOT (uppercase) as the canonical derivation. Accepting four unspecified lowercase aliases invites per-script divergence and defeats the gate's purpose of enforcing a single canonical pattern. Either document these aliases explicitly in the module docstring with a justification, or drop them and migrate any existing callers to REPO_ROOT.

findings_details.title: Module docstring lists fewer allowed prefixes than the implementation actually accepts
findings_details.body: The module docstring (lines 5-7) says bare references must be preceded by "$REPO_ROOT, $PROOF_DIR, $QUALITY_DIR, $(resolve_quality_dir), $(resolve_proof_dir), $(resolve_plans_dir)". The implementation (ROOTED_PREFIX_RE, lines 25-31) also accepts $METRICS_DIR and four lowercase aliases ($git_root, $repo_root, $local_root, $worktree_root). Future maintainers will read the docstring as ground truth and be confused when lines with $METRICS_DIR pass. Bring the docstring and regex into sync as a single source of truth — or derive ROOTED_PREFIX_RE from a named list constant documented at the top of the file.

findings_details.title: _is_ignored_path .quality guard matches absolute REPO_ROOT path but git ls-files returns relative paths
findings_details.body: Line 127 matches $1 against *"$REPO_ROOT"/.quality/* and "$REPO_ROOT"/.quality* . git ls-files (line 103) and git diff --name-only (line 111) emit repo-relative paths like .quality/plans/foo.md, never the absolute $REPO_ROOT/.quality/... form. The absolute-path guard will therefore never trigger in normal operation; excluding the .quality/ tree from the markdown gate is relying on this logic, so it silently fails open. Either strip $REPO_ROOT from the test (match .quality/* and .quality* directly) or add the relative form to the case statement above. Low-risk to misdiagnose as cosmetic — it directly affects whether the markdown gate scans files under .quality/.

findings_details.title: New scripts/lib/ directory hosts a single 40-line helper
findings_details.body: lib/ is a reasonable location for sourced helpers, but it is introduced here for a single file (quality-paths.sh, 40 lines). The existing scripts/ directory already contains other sourced helpers that follow a different convention: git-helpers.sh, cache.sh, load-config.sh, detect-test-runner.sh, test-file-patterns.sh, select-affected-tests.sh, run-subproject-tests.sh all live at scripts/ top level and are sourced directly. Introducing scripts/lib/ establishes a second convention. Either (a) move quality-paths.sh up to scripts/ to match the existing convention, or (b) accept lib/ as the new home and plan to migrate the other sourced helpers over time. Leaving it split 1-vs-N with no documented rationale is the worst of both worlds.

findings_details.title: Three separate python3 invocations to parse the violations array
findings_details.body: The gate wrapper shells out to python3 three times: line 41 to count violations, lines 56-74 as a heredoc to write the fail-proof JSON, and lines 77-82 to pretty-print violations to stderr. Each invocation re-parses the same violations_json string. A single python3 -c/-m call that takes the JSON once and emits count + pretty-print + writes the proof file would be clearer and avoid re-parsing. Alternatively, let gate_quality_path_discipline.py emit the full proof JSON directly and have the wrapper just tee it to the proof file and print to stderr — collapsing the Python/bash boundary down to one call.

findings_details.title: Bash test cases 1, 2, 9 re-cover ground already covered by the Python unit tests
findings_details.body: Case 1 (bare .sh FAIL) and Case 2 (rooted $REPO_ROOT PASS) in the .sh suite re-test what test_line_has_bare_quality_plain and test_line_has_bare_quality_rooted_repo_root already verify in the Python suite at the unit level. Case 9 explicitly invokes python3 "$SCANNER" directly, duplicating the scope of test_collect_violations_finds_sh_and_md. The bash suite's unique value is verifying the bash wrapper's exit code and proof-JSON format; cases that assert Python scanner behavior add no coverage and increase maintenance cost when the rooted-prefix rules evolve. Trim the .sh suite to wrapper-level concerns (exit codes, proof JSON shape, CHANGELOG/test_*.sh skipping, fallback-default handling as an end-to-end integration) and let the Python unit tests own the line-by-line detection rules.

findings_details.title: Fallback-default and setter checks whitelist the entire line instead of the specific occurrence
findings_details.body: Inside the per-occurrence loop at line 74, when a line matches FALLBACK_DEFAULT_RE (line 81) or SETTER_ASSIGNMENT_RE (line 84) the whole line is treated as clean. That means a single line like PROOF="${X:-.quality/a}" && cat .quality/b will whitelist both occurrences even though only the first is a fallback-default; the second is a bare reference that should be flagged. The fix is to scope the allow-check to the specific occurrence (test whether this particular .quality/ is the one preceded by the fallback/setter pattern) instead of matching anywhere on the line. As a design, the per-occurrence split strategy is correct — the fallback/setter checks just need to participate in the same per-occurrence scope.

findings_details.title: CHECKPOINT_DIR default uses bare relative .quality/checkpoints — passes gate only by setter-assignment exemption
findings_details.body: Line 8 defines CHECKPOINT_DIR="${CHECKPOINT_DIR:-.quality/checkpoints}". This happens to match SETTER_ASSIGNMENT_RE and FALLBACK_DEFAULT_RE so the gate passes, but it is the one place in the runtime where the new path-discipline rule is sidestepped via exemption rather than honored by derivation. For the flagship orchestrator script, derive the path from REPO_ROOT like every other script: CHECKPOINT_DIR="${CHECKPOINT_DIR:-$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)/.quality/checkpoints}" (or reuse the lib helper once you add resolve_checkpoint_dir). Leading by example matters more for run-gates.sh than for any other file.

findings_details.title: e2e test depends on real $HOME/.claude/plugins for hook_trace_log.py helper
findings_details.body: The test copies hook-trace-wrapper.sh into a mktemp sandbox but does NOT copy its sibling hook_trace_log.py. The wrapper at hook-trace-wrapper.sh:77-82 first looks for the helper next to itself (sandbox — absent), then falls back to $HOME/.claude/plugins/.../rq/.../hook_trace_log.py. Since the test never overrides HOME, it relies on the developer's real installed plugin. On a clean CI runner (no cached rq plugin), the fallback find returns empty, the `|| true` at wrapper:87 swallows the error, NO log line is written, and the test fails at line 40 with 'expected 1 log line with var set, got 0'. The test claims to be a full-stack integration test but actually short-circuits on an external precondition. Fix: either (a) copy hook_trace_log.py into the sandbox alongside the wrapper, or (b) set HOME=$SANDBOX and stage a fake plugin tree so the fallback has a deterministic target, or (c) make the wrapper's helper path an env var the test can override.

findings_details.title: Log rotation contradicts SKILL.md claim of 'keeping the last 25 MB'
findings_details.body: SKILL.md:67 documents: 'The log rotates when it exceeds 50 MB, keeping the last 25 MB.' The implementation at hook-trace-wrapper.sh:42-54 renames the full 50 MB file to '${LOG}.${ts}.bak' and starts a fresh log — it does NOT truncate to 25 MB, and it keeps EVERY rotated archive forever. After N rotations the user accumulates N × 50 MB of archived logs with no retention policy. Either fix the implementation (tail -c 25MB during rotation, or prune old .bak files) or fix the doc to say 'rotates full archives; oldest archives retained indefinitely'. The current state is a silent disk-usage bug + documentation lie.

findings_details.title: Env-gate test covers unset but not empty-string case
findings_details.body: The spec (review focus #5) requires the prefix to collapse when CLAUDE_RQ_TRACE_WRAPPER is 'set but empty'. Shell's ${var:+...} treats unset and empty-string identically, so behavior is correct, but check_prefix_collapse only exercises the unset case (line 83: `unset CLAUDE_RQ_TRACE_WRAPPER`). A regression that switched the expansion to ${var+...} (which expands when var is set, even to empty) would pass this test but break the empty-string contract. Add a parallel assertion with CLAUDE_RQ_TRACE_WRAPPER='' set explicitly, expecting the same empty result.

findings_details.title: Stdout and stderr are serialized instead of interleaved
findings_details.body: The wrapper captures OUT and ERR to separate tempfiles (line 69) and emits them sequentially at lines 90-91: `cat "$OUT"; cat "$ERR" >&2`. For a wrapped hook that writes to both streams, the consumer sees all stdout first and all stderr second, losing the original interleaving. Claude Code hooks typically emit a single JSON blob on stdout with diagnostic on stderr, so this is usually harmless — but hooks that print progress to stderr during long operations (e.g., `echo 'Checking...' >&2; do-work; echo '{...json...}'`) will have their ordering flattened. If strict ordering is not required, document it explicitly in the wrapper header. If it is required, tee through a single fd.

findings_details.title: Install silently overwrites existing wrapper and env file without confirmation or backup
findings_details.body: Lines 47-49 unconditionally `cp "$WRAPPER_SRC" "$WRAPPER_DST"` and rewrite ENV_DST. If a user has customized either file (e.g., wrapper with extra logging, env file with a different CLAUDE_RQ_TRACE_WRAPPER target pointing to a forked wrapper), those customizations are destroyed with no warning. At minimum, emit `overwriting existing hook-trace-wrapper.sh` to stderr when WRAPPER_DST already exists, or offer a `--force` flag and refuse by default. Same concern applies to ENV_DST at line 49.

findings_details.title: Log append does not enforce 0600 mode on pre-existing log files
findings_details.body: hook-trace-wrapper.sh:21-22 documents 'Log files are created with mode 0600 so stdout/stderr/tool_command content (which may contain secrets) is not world-readable.' The umask 077 (wrapper line 31) only applies to files CREATED by the wrapper process. If the log file already exists with wider permissions (e.g., a user ran an earlier version without umask, or the file was touched manually), `open(logfile, 'a')` at hook_trace_log.py:68 preserves the existing mode. The secrecy guarantee in the docs is therefore not enforced across upgrades. Fix: before appending, stat the log; if mode is wider than 0600, either chmod it to 0600 or refuse to write with a clear error. The wrapper could also run `chmod 600 "$LOG"` after ensuring it exists.

findings_details.title: Silent-wrong fallback when git is broken — gate passes with 0 violations
findings_details.body: `REPO_ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null || pwd)"` falls back to $PWD when git is unavailable or CWD is outside a git tree. The Python scanner then looks for $REPO_ROOT/plugins/rq, which does not exist, so collect_violations() returns [] and the gate reports PASS with 0 violations — silently masking a broken environment. The gate should fail closed (exit non-zero with 'not in a git tree' like lib/quality-paths.sh does) rather than report a clean pass. Recommended fix: if ! REPO_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null); then write a skip/error proof and exit non-zero, mirroring the discipline of lib/quality-paths.sh:resolve_quality_dir.

findings_details.title: FALLBACK_DEFAULT_RE and SETTER_ASSIGNMENT_RE short-circuit the whole line, allowing bare refs to hide alongside allowed patterns
findings_details.body: line_has_bare_quality() iterates per-occurrence of .quality/ but checks FALLBACK_DEFAULT_RE.search(line) and SETTER_ASSIGNMENT_RE.match(stripped) against the full line, not the specific occurrence. A line like `PROOF_DIR="${PROOF_DIR:-.quality/proof}"; cat .quality/proof/foo.json` contains BOTH a legitimate fallback default AND a bare usage; the fallback regex matches anywhere on the line, so every occurrence is whitelisted and the bare `cat .quality/proof/foo.json` passes. Same false-negative risk for the setter-assignment allowance (line 84): `PROOF_DIR=".quality/proof" && cat .quality/foo` is a single line in shell that line_has_bare_quality will pass. Fix: scope the two allow-patterns to the specific occurrence's prefix/substring rather than the whole line, or split by `;`/`&&` before scanning.

findings_details.title: ECHO_PROSE_RE false-negative: echo with command substitution containing real .quality/ path is wrongly treated as prose
findings_details.body: ECHO_PROSE_RE matches any line beginning with `echo`/`printf` followed by a quoted string containing `.quality/`. That covers `echo "See .quality/proof/ for output."` (intended) but also matches `echo "result: $(cat .quality/proof/foo.json)"` — the bare path inside the $() is a genuine usage, not prose, yet the whole line is suppressed. Fix: only skip prose when .quality/ is NOT inside a `$(...)` or backtick command substitution; or drop this allowance entirely and move the two intended prose lines (echo "See .quality/proof/" strings inside gate-quality-path-discipline.sh itself) into a narrow allow-list comment.

findings_details.title: Fence parser treats `~~~` fences as prose and mis-pairs nested backtick fences
findings_details.body: _iter_bash_fence_lines only matches ``` / ```` fences via `^(`{3,})(\w*)`. CommonMark also accepts `~~~` fences — any `~~~bash` block inside a SKILL.md would be parsed as prose, bypassing the scanner entirely. Additionally, when a 4-backtick fence encloses a 3-backtick example, the inner ``` toggles in_fence off prematurely. Both yield false-negatives. Fix: recognize `~~~` fences and remember the exact opening fence marker so only an equal-or-longer closing marker of the same char terminates the block.

findings_details.title: Bare fence (no language) is treated as bash — scans Python/diff/text snippets
findings_details.body: `if in_fence and fence_lang in ("bash", "sh", "")`. Bare ``` fences are considered bash for scanning purposes. This is exercised in tests (test_scan_md_file_bare_fence_treated_as_bash) so it is a deliberate choice, but it produces false-positives on markdown fences containing non-bash snippets that happen to mention .quality/. The design tension should be called out in the docstring; either document the convention 'bare fences are scanned, use ```text for plain-text examples' or narrow the language set to {bash, sh}.

findings_details.title: resolve_proof_dir / resolve_plans_dir have unexpected side effects (mkdir) for a `resolve_*` query API
findings_details.body: The naming convention `resolve_*` reads as a pure query; both functions silently mkdir on first call. A sourced read-only context (e.g., a helper that only needs the path for logging, or a sandbox with a read-only .quality/) will either fail under `set -e` (mkdir -p on a read-only FS returns non-zero) or succeed with a directory created as a side effect. The `mkdir -p` return value is also unchecked — if it fails, printf still emits the path and the caller receives a nonexistent directory without signal. Fix: either rename to `ensure_proof_dir` / `ensure_plans_dir` to advertise the side effect, or split into pure-query + explicit-ensure helpers, and propagate mkdir failure (`mkdir -p ... || return 1`).

findings_details.title: REPO_ROOT fallback to pwd causes silent no-op outside a git tree
findings_details.body: `REPO_ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null || pwd)"` — if git is unavailable, BASELINE_FILE anchors in pwd, _collect_files' `git ls-files` / `git diff` emit nothing, and the gate reports pass with file_count=0. The pattern is widespread in the codebase and this PR did not introduce it, but it is exactly the silent-wrong behavior that quality-path-discipline is meant to outlaw. Consider aligning gate-markdown.sh with lib/quality-paths.sh — fail fast when git is not available rather than masquerading as a clean pass.

findings_details.title: Glob pattern `"$REPO_ROOT"/.quality*` over-matches sibling directories like `.quality-backup/`
findings_details.body: In `[[ "$1" == *"$REPO_ROOT"/.quality/* || "$1" == "$REPO_ROOT"/.quality* ]]` the second arm uses a trailing `*` glob so `$REPO_ROOT/.qualityother` or `$REPO_ROOT/.quality-bak` also match. If a contributor ever creates such a sibling directory (e.g., `.quality-backup/`, `.qualityold/`) its markdown files will be silently skipped. Tighten to `"$REPO_ROOT"/.quality` (exact) or `"$REPO_ROOT"/.quality/*` if you want to catch files at any depth.

findings_details.title: `git rev-parse ... || pwd` fallback — same silent-wrong pattern
findings_details.body: `REPO_ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null || pwd)"` inside _fetch_plan_from_issue_comments falls back to pwd outside a git tree. The immediate `[ -f "$binding" ] || return 0` on line 93 makes this safe in practice (no binding → no-op), but the pattern is the same anti-pattern quality-path-discipline is trying to eliminate. Either remove the fallback or document why this specific site is intentionally lax.

findings_details.title: Scanner output captured without stderr isolation — scanner warnings corrupt JSON parsing
findings_details.body: `VIOLATIONS_JSON="$(python3 "$SCRIPT_DIR/gate_quality_path_discipline.py" "$REPO_ROOT")"` captures stdout only, which is correct. However if the scanner ever learns to emit progress to stdout (e.g., during a debug build), the next `json.loads(sys.argv[1])` on line 41 will raise and the ERR trap writes a generic crash proof that hides the real reason. Consider adding `set -o pipefail`-style guard: validate the captured string parses as JSON before passing it further, and write a more descriptive fail proof on JSON-parse errors.

findings_details.title: quality-path-discipline has no _gate_patterns entry — cache invalidates on every file change
findings_details.body: `quality-path-discipline` is registered at line 61 but not added to the `_gate_patterns` case statement (lines 113-128). It falls through to the default `*` clause, which invalidates the cache for ANY changed file — including unrelated doc-only or JSON edits. This is conservative (fails safe by running the gate too often rather than too rarely) but means the gate will re-run on virtually every commit, negating cache benefits. Consider adding a `quality-path-discipline) echo '*.sh *.md' ;;` entry so caching tracks only the file types the scanner actually reads.

findings_details.title: create_issue fallback parses issue number from unstructured output with `grep -oE '[0-9]+$' | tail -1`
findings_details.body: When `gh issue create --json` fails (non-zero exit — gh may still have created the issue before failing), create_issue falls back to parsing the human-readable URL with `grep -oE '[0-9]+$' | tail -1`. The regex matches any trailing digits on any line, so an auth-warning banner like `Gist ID: 123` or a stderr line bleeding into stdout could yield the wrong number. Also, running `gh issue create` a second time on the fallback path would create a duplicate issue if the first partially succeeded. Fix: detect the exact failure modes of the first call, or only fall back when gh exit code indicates 'not fatal' (no issue created).

findings_details.title: grep -rlF on $HOME/.claude/plans follows into symlinks and fails noisily if dir missing
findings_details.body: `grep -rlF "Branch: $branch" "$plans_dir"` — if $HOME/.claude/plans contains symlinks to other repos' plan dirs, grep recurses into all of them and may surface cross-project plans (a privacy-adjacent leak in multi-tenant setups). Also, if $plans_dir does not exist, grep prints 'No such file' to stderr; the `2>/dev/null || true` mutes that but masks a genuine misconfiguration. Consider `find -maxdepth N -not -type l` or explicit directory creation on first use.

findings_details.title: source-ing git-helpers.sh from $(find ...) subshell leaks no helpers back
findings_details.body: `source "$(find . -name 'git-helpers.sh' -path '*/rq/*' 2>/dev/null | head -1)"` — if the find returns empty (cwd isn't the plugin checkout), the source arg is empty and `source ""` fails under set -e in a typical script; in this SKILL.md bash fence it just emits an error. Also, if the find returns multiple candidates (worktree + cache), `head -1` may pick the stale one. The step works today because the script runs from the plugin checkout, but the preamble convention elsewhere in the repo uses the PLUGIN_DIR discovery twice (local then $HOME/.claude). Apply the same two-step fallback here.

findings_details.title: `git push --force-with-lease` without an explicit rev argument
findings_details.body: The snippet shows `git push --force-with-lease` with no ref argument. In recent git (2.30+), `--force-with-lease` without an argument protects against the remote having advanced since last fetch, but if an upstream commit was fetched into refs/remotes and then force-pushed-over by another agent, the lease check still passes. For a stricter invariant, use `--force-with-lease=<ref>:<expected>` pinning to the local ref the author last pulled. Minor — probably acceptable for a single-author feature branch.

findings_details.title: Agent prompt assumes `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` always succeeds
findings_details.body: `REPO_ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"` has no fallback — under `set -e` (not explicitly set in the fenced block) or via the subagent's shell, a non-git cwd will cause the write to /.quality/proof/... at filesystem root, or fail silently depending on the wrapper. Consider failing loud: `REPO_ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null)" || { echo 'critic: not in a git tree' >&2; exit 1; }`.
